headline: Endurance.
Resilience.
Propulsion.
blocks.subhead: A ground-up identity for Battle Road, built from the name outward, through mind-mapping, dozens of logo directions and a mark that had to work as well on a trade-show stand as it does on a business card.

blocks.label: The brief
heading: Distilled through
iteration.
paragraphs: Battle Road needed an identity from nothing, a name with no visual language attached yet. Rather than jump to a mark, the process started with the name itself: what does "Battle" suggest, what does "Road" suggest, and where do the two overlap.

blocks.label: The idea
heading: Endurance,
mapped.
paragraphs: The mind map split into four territories. "Battle" pulled toward soldiers, muskets, sabres and cannon. "Road" pulled toward aerial perspective, winding lines and streetlights. Where they met sat two more: simulation (worlds, nodes, grids, massive crowds) and digital (pixels, LEDs, glitch). Dozens of logo directions followed, a horse and rider, a lightning-struck flag, wordmark-only treatments, a bullet-holed license plate, before the peregrine falcon won out. It carries speed, precision and an aerial vantage point that ties the whole map back together.

Topographic contour lines became the identity's second signature, used across the brand book, business cards, banners and environmental graphics. They read as terrain and elevation, aerial and tactical at once, which is exactly the register the mark needed to hold.

blocks.text: The final lockup, falcon logomark beside a tall condensed wordmark, built to sit cleanly above the topographic linework used throughout the rest of the system.

blocks.caption: Topographic 3D terrain, rendered as the brand book's opening scene.

blocks.label: The result
heading: Engineered
for speed.
paragraphs: The finished system was built for flexibility from the start, the same falcon and contour-line language carrying across business cards, roll-up banners, apparel and a full-scale wall installation, all built around the tagline "Create Your Own Journey."

Battle Road's work has since evolved into AtomEngine, a real-time simulation platform used for defence, infrastructure and training. The falcon's aerial vantage and the topographic linework, chosen for entirely different reasons at the time, turned out to describe that direction rather well.

blocks.caption: The identity, built out in 3D for Battle Road's stand at I/ITSEC, the defence simulation industry's largest annual event.

blocks.label: Where it landed
heading: A quiet startup,
no longer quiet.
paragraphs: Wargaming and defence simulation is a staid market, thick with grey vendor logos and government-issue PDFs. A brand built with real presence, on the show floor at I/ITSEC and everywhere else it showed up, gave Battle Road a way to stand out from that crowd and be taken seriously at the same time.

In January 2026, Battle Road was acquired by OneBrief in a deal announced alongside a $2.15 billion Series D. OneBrief's own announcement called Battle Road's wargaming software "in broad use across the U.S. and allied militaries" and cited its selection as the core simulation engine for the US Army's Next Generation Constructive programme, a rare position for a seed-stage startup to hold.
